TigerGraph 4.1.4 · High-Availability QA investigation

What survives a node failure — and where the cluster falls off a cliff

A depth-first resilience test of a 3-node TigerGraph HA cluster. The data is replicated across two nodes, but the coordination quorum tolerates only one loss — so effective fault tolerance is one node, set by the quorum layer, not by data replication.

3×
node HA cluster
RF 2
data replication
45
checks (31+8+6)
1
node fault tolerance
~500k
edges under test
01 · Environment

A real cluster, built to fail on purpose

Why self-managed Enterprise, and what's under test.

The brief asked for a HA node-failure test. Community Edition (single-server, no clustering) and the managed Savanna cloud (no node-level failure control) can't satisfy that, so the system under test is a self-managed TigerGraph 4.1.4 Enterprise cluster with an HA-enabled license, built to exercise real node loss.

4.1.3 was requested; TigerGraph no longer distributes it, so 4.1.4 (the current 4.1 LTS) was used deliberately.

SoftwareTigerGraph 4.1.4 Enterprise · HA · replication factor 2
Nodes3× GCP e2-standard-4 (4 vCPU / 16 GB) · Ubuntu 22.04 · 50 GB SSD
Zonesingle zone us-central1-a
Data20k Person · 50k Account · 50k OWNS · ~500k TRANSFER edges
Workloadspoint-lookup · 3-hop traversal · write path
02 · Architecture

Two planes that fail differently

The mental model behind every finding.

TigerGraph has two planes that fail differently. Almost every finding follows from this split — reads and writes live on a leaderless replicated data plane; schema, installs and session coordination live on a leader-plus-quorum control plane.

Data plane — GPE / GSE / RESTPPControl plane — GSQL leader, ZK/etcd
Architectureleaderless, replicated (RF 2)leader / follower + quorum majority
On a 1-node lossreads keep serving (brief reroute)GSQL fails over to a standby; GUI stays up
Blocked while degraded—INSTALL QUERY, schema change (by design)
Hard limitsurvives 1 replica lossquorum needs 2 of 3 — tolerates only 1 loss
The quorum row is the whole story: data replication would let a single surviving node answer everything, but the coordination quorum won't form with only one node up — so the cluster stops well before the data does.
03 · Findings

Six failure scenarios, measured

Downtime and mean-time-to-recovery for each injected failure.

CaseScenarioResultMTTR
1a/1bKill / power off a non-data nodereads unaffected (100%) — transparent ok0 s
1cKill a data-replica nodereads 99.5% — brief reroute, retry-maskable degrades~18–21 s
2Kill the GSQL leadercontrol plane pauses; reads/writes unaffected degrades~58 s
3INSTALL / schema change while degradedblocked by design; un-blocks on recovery degradesheal ~32 s
4Quorum boundary (2nd node down)cluster unusable — writes rejected, reads flap — despite a full replica critical~40–60 s
5Writes during a data-node failureatomic & consistent — 1994 ok = 1994 persisted okre-sync ~18 s
04 · Headline

The quorum boundary

Data present, cluster unusable.

With three nodes the cluster serves everything. Take one node down and it self-heals. Take a second node down and it falls off a cliff — even though the last surviving node holds a complete copy of the data. That gap between "data present" and "cluster usable" is the finding.

3 / 3 nodes
Fully healthy
reads + writes serve normally; replicas identical
writes100%
2 / 3 nodes
Degraded, still serving
1 node down — quorum intact (2 of 3); reads reroute, writes commit
writes~ok
1 / 3 nodes
Quorum lost — unusable
2 down — no quorum majority; writes rejected, reads flap, despite a full data replica on the survivor
writesrejected
m2 up  200 200 200 200 200 200 200 200  ← m2 powered off →  000 000 200 000 200 200 200 200 200 200

read-probe HTTP status through a data-node power-off — a brief 000 burst, then steady 200s (Case 1c)

Honesty note. An initial pass reported the boundary as a clean "0% reads." A finer-grained re-measurement showed reads actually flap ~50/50 for minutes before fully failing — the earlier figure was a too-short-window artefact. Writes, which fail deterministically, are the cleaner signal of quorum loss. The report documents the corrected behaviour.
05 · Detail

The cases, one by one

Case 1a / 1b

Kill or power off a non-data node (m3)

reads 100% · transparent
m3 holds no data replica, so removing it is transparent — the read probe never dropped a request.
MTTR 0 s
Case 1c

Kill a data-replica node (m2)

reads 99.5% · retry-maskable
Reads keep serving from the surviving replica after a brief reroute. The blip is a handful of failed requests over ~20 s — fully maskable with a client retry.
MTTR ~18–21 s
Case 2

Kill the GSQL leader

data plane unaffected
The control plane pauses while a standby is elected — but reads and writes on the data plane are unaffected throughout. The election shows up verbatim in the real GsqlHAHandler log.
MTTR ~58 s
Case 3

Schema change / INSTALL QUERY while degraded

blocked by design
DDL is correctly blocked while a node is down. Note the trap: the CLI prints a success-looking message, but the query only reaches (pendingInstall) and the REST endpoint 404s — you must verify the effect, not trust the CLI.
MTTR heal ~32 s
Case 5

Writes during a data-node failure

atomic · no loss/dupes
2,000 writes were driven through a node failure: 1,994 returned success and exactly 1,994 persisted — no loss, no duplicates. Replicas re-synced byte-identical after catch-up.
MTTR re-sync ~18 s
06 · Testing

Functional & database coverage

31 automated + 8 destructive/manual + 6 HA = 45 checks.

Three different kinds of check, kept distinct on purpose — calling them one homogeneous "test" number would overstate what runs automatically.

31
Automated pytest tests
FT1–12 functional (CRUD, queries, traversal, aggregation, filtering) · DT1–7 database correctness · MD1–12 messy / larger data. Fast, safe, CI-able — pytest -v.
8
Destructive / manual checks
CR1–4 consistency after node failure+recovery · BR1–4 backup & restore. They stop cloud VMs, so they run as operator procedures, not inside the pytest suite.
6
HA / resilience scenarios
The node-failure investigation above, each with measured downtime and MTTR.

pytest run reports   functional + database — 19 passed · messy / larger data — 12 passed · suite source tests/ · full matrix TEST-CASES.md

08 · Evidence

The cluster in its own tools

Genuine screenshots — terminal and Admin Portal.

The system under test was a genuine 3-node HA cluster — here it is in its own tools. Click any image to enlarge.

09 · Recommendations

What to do with this

✓ What worked as expected

  • Data-plane HA — reads survive a replica loss with only a brief, retry-maskable reroute.
  • Write consistency — writes are atomic; every acknowledged write persisted, no duplicates.
  • Functional correctness — 31/31 automated tests pass, including precision and messy data.
  • Backup / restore — clean point-in-time revert of the whole store.
  • Self-healing coordination — GSQL leader and replicas recover automatically on rejoin.

⚠ What needs improvement

  • ~58 s GSQL failover — the control-plane pause is long enough to notice.
  • The quorum cliff — 3 nodes tolerate only one loss; a 3-node cluster is not "2-failure safe."
  • No auto-start — services don't come back after a node reboot without gadmin start.
  • Misleading CLI success — INSTALL reports success while the query is only pendingInstall.
  • Disruptive restore — restore stops the data/query services; it's planned-maintenance only.

Actionable recommendations

  1. Wrap reads in a client retry to mask the ~20 s reroute window.
  2. Use ≥5 nodes if you need to tolerate two simultaneous node losses.
  3. Run RF=3 where the extra replica is affordable, for stronger data durability.
  4. Add systemd auto-start so services rejoin after a reboot unattended.
  5. Monitor writes and gadmin status, not just reads — writes are the true quorum signal.
  6. Schedule DDL / INSTALL for healthy windows; they're blocked while degraded.
  7. Verify the effect, not the CLI — check the REST endpoint after an INSTALL.
  8. Treat restore as maintenance — it stops services; plan a window.
TigerGraph 4.1.4 HA — Failure & Recovery Testing
A depth-first QA investigation. Full report, pytest suite, probe CSVs, logs and all screenshots in the repo.
Evidence is genuine: every screenshot is a real capture from a live end-to-end run; terminal output was recorded to a session log and each command tees full output. 45 checks total — 31 automated pytest, 8 destructive/manual, 6 HA scenarios.
